prompt: What SPECIFICALLY makes cohere.com (Cohere, the AI company) read as a premium unicorn brand in 2025-2026? I need designer-grade detail, not vibes: the exact typefaces (and foundries) used for display vs body, the type scale and letter-spacing choices, the precise palette (hex values or close approximations designers cite), how imagery/illustration is art-directed (3D renders? editorial photography? abstract systems diagrams?), grid and whitespace decisions, nav and footer construction, and any signature motion. Quote designers/design reviews verbatim (@handle + date) on X and cite teardowns, Fonts In Use entries, awards pages, or agency case studies (which agency/studio designed it?). Every claim must be specific enough that a developer could replicate the move.

Deconstruct crusoe.ai's website design (Crusoe, the AI/energy cloud company) in implementable detail: how is its cinematic dark 'energy' aesthetic actually built? Specifics needed: hero construction (video? WebGL? layered gradients? photography treatment?), the dark palette (approximate hex values, how pure-black vs tinted-dark), typography (faces, weights, sizes), how light/glow/atmosphere is rendered, scroll behavior and motion easing, section rhythm, and what studio/agency built it. Quote designer praise or critique verbatim from X (@handle + date) and cite case studies, Awwwards/Godly entries, or teardowns. I want technique names and values a front-end dev could copy, not adjectives.

Deconstruct mecka.ai's website design AND identify the other 2025-2026 AI startup marketing sites designers on X call 'legendary', 'insane', 'best site I've seen', or similar superlatives. For mecka.ai and each named site: what concretely earns the praise — typography choices (exact faces), color/material treatment, hero technique (WebGL/video/illustration), layout geometry, motion design — and who designed it. Quote the verbatim praise posts (@handle + date) and cite Godly/Awwwards/SiteInspire features. Output should be a ranked list of named sites with the 2-3 specific replicable moves each one makes. No vague 'clean and modern' descriptions — values, techniques, structure.

Define the 2026 shared design language of top AI infrastructure brands: anthropic.com, scale.com, together.ai, groq.com, modal.com (plus baseten, fal.ai if relevant). Across these sites, what are the SHARED moves designers identify: type systems (the serif-revival vs neo-grotesque split — which exact faces: Styrene? Tiempos? Suisse? ABC Diatype? GT America?), background materials (warm paper vs cold dark vs gradient mesh), illustration/diagram languages, border-and-hairline usage, card and code-block styling, and density/whitespace norms. Cite Fonts In Use, brand-refresh announcements, agency credits, and verbatim X commentary (@handle + date). Conclude with a concrete 'house style of AI infra 2026' spec: faces, weights, spacing, palette tendencies, materials — implementable by a dev.

What concretely separates a TEMPLATE-LOOKING dark SaaS marketing site from an AWARD-TIER dark site in 2025-2026? I want the specific tells designers list when they critique: e.g., pure #000 vs tinted near-blacks, default border-radius and shadow values, generic purple-gradient glows, stock Tailwind/shadcn component look, uniform section padding, centered-everything layouts, fake bento grids — versus what award-tier sites do instead (custom type, real lighting models, asymmetric grids, bespoke iconography, intentional grain/texture, motion with crafted easing). Quote design-Twitter critiques and teardowns verbatim (@handle + date) and cite articles/videos. For every 'template tell' give the concrete fix with values (hex, px, easing curves, structure). This should read as an actionable checklist.

Catalog SPECIFIC WebGL/canvas hero implementations on real production marketing sites in 2025-2026 that designers and devs publicly praised. For each: the site name, what the hero is (named technique: raymarched shader, FBO particle field, mesh/animated gradient, fluid simulation, ASCII shader, point-cloud morph, noise-displaced plane, dithered gradient), the library used (Three.js, react-three-fiber, OGL, regl, raw WebGL2, or pure canvas 2D), performance approach (DPR capping, IntersectionObserver pausing, reduced-motion fallback), and where the technique is documented (Codrops tutorial, Maxime Heckel / @MaximeHeckel posts, shader breakdowns). Quote praise/breakdown posts verbatim (@handle + date). I want a menu of named, implementable hero techniques with their canonical tutorials.

Document the typography systems of unicorn-tier AI startup sites in 2025-2026 with EXACT specifics: which typefaces (and foundries — Commercial Type, Klim, ABC Dinamo, Grilli, OH no, General Type), display vs text pairings, hero sizes in px/rem, line-height and letter-spacing values (e.g., -0.02em tracking on large display), weight usage, and use of monospace as a brand voice. Cover the 2026 serif-vs-grotesk trend: which AI brands moved to editorial serifs (who, which face) and which doubled down on neo-grotesks. Cite Fonts In Use pages, brand-launch posts, and verbatim designer commentary on X (@handle + date). Conclude with 3 concrete, named type-stack recipes (display face + text face + mono) that currently read as frontier-2026, with sizing/tracking values.

Color and material trends for premium DARK websites in 2026, in implementable terms: (1) gradients — what kinds read premium now (mesh, oklch-interpolated, dithered, subtle radial vignettes) vs dated (linear purple-to-blue); (2) grain/noise — how it's applied (SVG feTurbulence, tiled PNG, shader), at what opacity, blend mode; (3) glass/blur — current refined usage vs the dated glassmorphism look; (4) 'metallic ink' / foil / iridescent accents — who does it and how (gradient text fills, masked shaders); (5) simulated lighting on dark surfaces — top-edge highlights, radial key lights, vignettes, tinted shadows — with example hex/rgba/blur values. For each, cite real sites praised for it, designer posts verbatim (@handle + date), and tutorials. Values and recipes, not adjectives.

Scroll-driven storytelling and motion design on top-tier marketing sites in 2025-2026 — the implementable state of the art: which libraries top sites actually use (GSAP ScrollTrigger post-free-license, Motion (motion.dev), Lenis smooth scroll) and which approaches designers now AVOID (scroll-jacking, Locomotive-style hijacking, excessive parallax); adoption of native CSS scroll-driven animations (animation-timeline: view()/scroll()) and the View Transitions API on real sites; canonical patterns (pinned sections, scrub-linked canvas sequences, text mask reveals, sticky-stacked cards) with the sites that do them well; standard easing/duration conventions (e.g., 0.6-0.8s, custom cubic-bezier or expo-out) and reduced-motion handling. Quote practitioner posts verbatim (@handle + date) and cite talks/articles. Give me named patterns + the exact technique to build each.

List the Awwwards SOTD/Site-of-the-Month, Godly, and CSS Design Awards winning or featured DARK B2B, developer-tool, infrastructure, or SECURITY sites from 2025-2026. For each winner: site name + URL, the studio/designer credited, and SPECIFICALLY what won — the hero technique, type system, motion signature, material treatment — per the jury notes or the designer's own breakdown. Prioritize anything security/trust/forensics/crypto-adjacent. Quote award citations or designer posts verbatim (@handle + date) with links. The output should be a study list: 8-15 named sites with the one or two replicable moves each contributes.

How do the best trust/security/forensics/cryptography brands make evidence and crypto concepts BEAUTIFUL on the web in 2025-2026? Find praised examples of: visualizing signatures/hashes/attestation chains (how do they render a hash so it looks designed, not dumped?), audit-log and chain-of-custody visualizations, certificate/receipt/document metaphors done elegantly, terminal-and-monospace aesthetics elevated to brand level, and security companies (e.g., Doppler, Tailscale, Teleport, 1Password, Chainguard, Oso, Socket) whose visual systems designers praise. For each example: the concrete technique (layout, type, color, motion) and verbatim praise (@handle + date) or case study link. I'm designing an 'evidence room' aesthetic for a security attestation product — give me replicable patterns.

Collect designer CRITIQUES of the 'black background + neon green accent' (and neon-anything-on-black) website formula: why does it read as crypto-template / hacker-cliché / AI-slop in 2025-2026? Quote the critiques verbatim (@handle + date). Then, crucially, the prescriptions: what do designers say FIXES a dark site that needs a technical/security feel without the cliché — e.g., desaturating the accent, shifting accent hue, tinting the black (toward what hue and how much), introducing a second material (paper, light section), restrained accent usage ratios (accent on <5% of surface), replacing glows with real lighting, typography quality over color tricks. Each prescription must come with concrete values or rules and a cited source. This is a remediation guide, not a rant.

Document the layout GEOMETRY of premium marketing sites in 2025-2026 with concrete patterns: asymmetric grid usage (e.g., 12-col with content offset to cols 2-7, rail labels in col 1), full-bleed bands alternating with contained sections, editorial rhythm (how award-tier sites vary section height and density instead of uniform py-24 blocks), oversized numerals and index numbering (01/02/03 section markers, giant stat numerals — at what sizes), hairline rule systems and bordered table layouts, sticky side rails, and max-width conventions (1200? 1440? full + inner grid?). Cite specific sites doing each pattern, designer breakdowns verbatim (@handle + date), and articles. Output: a catalog of named layout patterns with their structural recipe (columns, spans, spacing values).

Find founders/designers describing the SINGLE highest-leverage visual investment a small team made that transformed how premium their site/brand was perceived — e.g., 'we licensed one great display typeface', 'we commissioned one 3D hero from an artist', 'we replaced stock illustration with a custom diagram language', 'we hired a studio for a 2-week brand sprint', 'we added a real lighting model to our dark theme', 'we rebuilt the hero in WebGL'. Quote the stories verbatim (@handle + date) with before/after detail and, where stated, cost and time. Rank the interventions by leverage (perception change per effort) based on the evidence. For each, give the concrete implementation path a 1-2 person team could execute. Cited, specific, no platitudes.
